reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a need to investigate and possibly address performance issues in microbenchmarks, no clear resolution or promise of fix
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for further investigation
reviews.raw_body:
I get it, I'll trace the microbenchmarks to see what happens there, 
thanks for the suggestion.

Thanks again,

Alex

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt questioned whether all entries in the obj_exts array are for the same memcg, and if not, why they're being charged to a single memcg; he also asked about the allocation behavior of alloc_percpu() and its variants, specifically whether they allocate memory on a single node or per local node.
reviews.raw_body:
Will all the entries in obj_exts array be for the same memcg? If not then why we
are charging the whole array to the one which happen to allocate the array?

Sorry I don't know the details of percpu allocator, so asking some dumb
questions:

1. Does the alloc_percpu() (& similar functions) allocate the underlying on a
   single node or does it allocate memory for each cpu on their local node?
   For slub, it is on the same node, so the situation is easier to handle.

2. On a typical system how much memory is consumed by obj_exts for the percpu
   allocator chunks? I am wondering if we don't charge it, how much will we
   loose?

3. What would be side effect on assuming that obj_exts is on the same node as
   the given chunk?

2026-05-21.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2

Author acknowledged a mistake in their previous patch where chunk-allocating-memcg would be charged for all metadata, which represents 64kB and is not negligible.

The author is addressing a concern about whether pcpu_alloc_pages() and pcpu_alloc_chunk() guarantee allocation on the CPU local node, explaining that while pcpu_alloc_pages() tries to allocate pages on the local node, it may still fallback to another node, and that pcpu_alloc_chunk() uses kmalloc or vmalloc which are not node-specific. The author does not plan a fix for this issue.

Author acknowledges a significant amount of memory is not being accounted for, specifically 2.3kB in dynamically allocated chunk fields, and asks if it's acceptable to lose this amount for code simplicity.

Author acknowledged a potential issue with overcharging one node while undercharging others due to obj_exts size, agreed to restructure and 'fix that' in the next version.
sentiment_signals: no guarantee of allocation on CPU local node
GFP_ACCOUNT is intended to handle non-node-specific allocations
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Shakeel,

On 5/21/26 19:25, Shakeel Butt wrote:

---

Hmm, I overlooked the amount allocated, so that's my mistake: the 
chunk-allocating-memcg will be charged for all the metadata, although 
before the charge was distributed. And according to Claude, the metadata 
would represent 64kB, so not negligible.

---

To me, chunk metadata and actual pages are allocated differently:

- pcpu_alloc_pages() tries to allocate the pages on the cpu local node 
https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.0.9/source/mm/percpu-vm.c#L95. But 
to me no guarantee it won't fallback to any other node. And I don't 
think that __GFP_THISNODE would be a good idea here.

- pcpu_alloc_chunk() uses kmalloc or vmalloc depending on the size, so 
not attached to specific node, that's why I wanted GFP_ACCOUNT to do the 
job for us in the first place.

---

So according to my previous answer, 64kB. I have just noticed that a 
bunch of dynamically allocated chunk fields are not accounted either, 
which again according to Claude represent 2.3kB. I don't have much 
experience in accounting but that's far from negligible right? Which 
amount are we keen to lose to make the code simpler (or for other reasons)?

---

Given the size of obj_exts, overcharging one node while undercharging 
others?

To conclude, you're right, I did not dive deep enough into the metadata 
sizes, I'll fix that.

Thanks,

Alex

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
reviews.summary: Author is seeking clarification on what currently ensures the lifetime of an object cgroup, specifically the one returned by current_obj_cgroup().
reviews.raw_body:
Sashiko raised the same question, so I need to be educated here: what 
currently guarantees the life objcg returned by current_obj_cgroup()?

2026-05-22.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.
